notes.title: marketing strategies
notes.content:
A field manual for revenue-focused operators, not marketers

PART I: CASE ANALYSES
Case 1: Dollar Shave Club — Launch Video (2012)
Context. Razor market dominated by Gillette (70% share), Schick, BIC. Razors were premium-priced commodities — locked behind retail glass, marketed via celebrity endorsement. Mike Dubin had $4,500 in venture funding and a subscription model. No retail distribution, no brand recognition, no path to scale via traditional channels.
Strategy. Bypass the entire CPG playbook. Rather than fight Gillette on shelf space and TV advertising, attack a structural weakness: Gillette charged $4-6 per razor cartridge. The pitch wasn't better razors — it was the racket was insulting to the customer. The campaign positioned the buyer as smart and the incumbent as treating them as stupid.
Execution. Single 90-second video. Founder on-camera in a warehouse, walking through product while making jokes about competitor pricing ("Our blades are f***ing great"). Distributed via the company's own social channels, seeded to tech press. No paid amplification at launch.
Mechanism of success. Three forces compounded:

Permission marketing through honesty. Stating openly that competitor pricing was a ripoff gave viewers permission to feel smart for switching. Most marketing tells customers the product is good. This told customers their previous choice was foolish — a much stronger psychological lever.
Asymmetric cost structure. Subscription meant the company captured LTV, not single-transaction revenue, justifying paying for traffic that wouldn't pay back on first purchase.
Founder-led credibility. Pre-influencer era. A founder making jokes about his own warehouse was novel. It read as transparent, which was the inverse of how CPG companies operated.

Outcomes. 12,000 orders in 48 hours. Site crashed. By 2016, $240M revenue. Acquired by Unilever for $1B.
Extracted playbook. When attacking an incumbent, don't position your product as better — position the incumbent's pricing/practice as insulting to the buyer. Works only when (a) there's a genuine pricing gap or practice you can credibly call out, (b) the founder can be on-camera credibly, and (c) the economic model can absorb high CAC. Fails when the founder isn't compelling, when the gap isn't real, or when single-transaction economics need to work.

Case 2: Notion — Reddit + Twitter Driven Growth (2018-2020)
Context. Productivity tool market saturated (Evernote, OneNote, Confluence, Coda). Notion had a unique product (flexible blocks, no rigid structure) but flexibility is a marketing curse — you can't describe it in a tagline. They had no sales team and refused to spend on traditional ads through their growth phase.
Strategy. Make users build the marketing. The product itself was the asset. Notion gave power users a free Personal Pro plan in exchange for templates they'd share publicly. Templates became distributed content — each one tagged "Built in Notion."
Execution. Three channels:

Reddit (r/Notion, r/productivity, r/StudentTip) — community moderation and engagement, not advertising. The Notion team participated in threads.
Twitter — power users (Ali Abdaal, Thomas Frank, Matt D'Avella) building personal brands using Notion templates as content.
YouTube — long-form tutorials by users, not Notion's marketing team.

No traditional funnel. The "funnel" was: see template → copy template → become user → build own template → share → repeat.
Mechanism of success.

Curation as marketing. Users showing off their personal Notion setup is identity signaling. The product became an extension of how productive/aesthetic someone wanted to appear. Templates were Instagram for productivity nerds.
Network effect through specificity. A generic productivity tool has no template economy. Notion's blocks-as-Lego architecture meant templates were both useful and distinct — every shared template was free advertising with a unique fingerprint.
Zero-friction onboarding. "Duplicate" button on every shared template removed the activation gap. Most SaaS dies at signup. Notion converted at the copy-paste moment.

Outcomes. $0 to $10B valuation in ~3 years. 30M+ users by 2023. CAC near zero through 2021. Maintained 90%+ organic acquisition until Series C.
Extracted playbook. Product-led growth works when the product has shareable artifacts (templates, dashboards, configurations) that serve dual purposes — useful to the creator, useful to the audience as marketing. Fails when the product doesn't produce artifacts worth sharing, or when the artifacts are generic (most B2B SaaS).

Case 3: Liquid Death — Mountain Water (2019-Present)
Context. Bottled water is the definition of commoditised. Aquafina, Dasani, Smart Water, store brands. Price points $1-3. Margins compressed. No differentiation possible on product attributes (water is water). Mike Cessario, an ex-creative director, raised $2M to launch... canned water.
Strategy. Position bottled water as identity-tribal, not functional. Target an audience the entire beverage industry ignored: people who don't drink alcohol but still want the aesthetic of drinking alcohol. Skateboarders, metal fans, fitness-adjacent edgy demographics. The tagline: "Murder Your Thirst."
Execution. Heavy metal aesthetic — black cans, skull motifs, horror-movie ad campaigns. Distribution via:

Whole Foods, 7-Eleven (placement adjacent to energy drinks, not water)
Instagram, TikTok organic content (videos of metalheads drinking water sincerely)
Brand partnerships with skate brands, music festivals, MMA

Ads parodied conventional water marketing — "Death to Plastic" environmental angle layered on top.
Mechanism of success.

Category re-categorisation. Water shelf placement = commodity competition. Liquid Death sat in premium beverages, mentally and physically. Customers bought a tribal identity item priced like a beer ($2-3) instead of a commodity ($0.50-1).
Demographic arbitrage. Sober-curious + metal/skate aesthetic was an untapped demographic. Every other water brand fought for the same "wellness mom" customer.
Cost structure flip. Marketing budget went into content production (low marginal cost, high virality) instead of media buying. A single TikTok of a 4-year-old metalhead drinking Liquid Death did 30M views with zero spend.

Outcomes. $0 to $263M revenue by 2024. $1.4B valuation. Maintained 30%+ gross margins on water — unheard of in the category.
Extracted playbook. When a category is commoditised, the only escape is to exit the category mentally. Re-categorise into something with brand permission to charge premium prices. Works when (a) there's a demographic the incumbents are ignoring, (b) the cost structure can support content-as-marketing, (c) you can credibly own the new category. Fails when the founder doesn't understand the subculture authentically (most attempts at this fail because the aesthetic feels contrived).

Case 4: Apple — iPhone Launch (2007)
Context. Smartphone market was Blackberry (business), Nokia (mass market), Palm Treo, Windows Mobile. All had physical keyboards. Apple was a music/computer company with zero phone industry credibility, no carrier relationships, and was viewed as too premium for mass adoption. Steve Jobs had to convince AT&T to give Apple revenue share on subscriptions — unprecedented.
Strategy. Don't launch a phone. Launch three devices that happen to be one device. The famous keynote framing — "An iPod, a phone, an internet communicator" — was strategic positioning, not just dramatic structure. It anchored value at $1,500 (three devices) for a $499 product. Frame as a category invention, not a competitor product.
Execution.

Single keynote with Jobs walking through the device for 90 minutes. No advertising for 6 months prior. No leaks.
Carrier exclusivity (AT&T) created scarcity and queue dynamics.
Launch day queues — generated media coverage worth $400M in equivalent ad spend.
No traditional retail launch — Apple Stores only, generating destination shopping behavior.

Mechanism of success.

Demonstration as proof. Most consumer electronics launches are spec sheets. The keynote was a demo. Jobs swiped, pinched, zoomed — the product sold itself by being shown working. This was the highest-converting "ad" in tech history.
Anchor pricing through bundling psychology. Three-device framing made $499 feel cheap. Without this, $499 would have been a price-point death zone (above commodity, below luxury).
Manufactured scarcity. Carrier exclusivity + queue dynamics + limited initial supply turned buyers into evangelists. Owning an iPhone in July 2007 was status signaling.

Outcomes. 270,000 units in first weekend. 6.1M units in first year. Defined the smartphone category — every subsequent phone was measured against this device for a decade.
Extracted playbook. Demonstrations convert better than descriptions. Bundle pricing reframes value perception. Scarcity creates evangelism. Works when (a) you have a genuinely new product worth demonstrating, (b) you can control initial distribution, (c) you have a credible founder/spokesperson. Fails for incremental products (most iPhone-launch parodies fail because the product isn't novel enough to demonstrate).

Case 5: Shopify — Build A Business Competition (2010-2017)
Context. Shopify needed to acquire merchants. Competitors (BigCommerce, WooCommerce, Magento) were equally capable products. Differentiation was nearly impossible on features. Marketing budget vs incumbents was 1/10th.
Strategy. Don't market the platform. Manufacture proof. Build a competition where entrepreneurs build businesses on Shopify in 6 months, with the winner receiving $500K and a private mentoring session with Tony Robbins, Tim Ferriss, Daymond John, and others.
Execution.

Annual competition with public leaderboard.
Mentor recruitment from the founder-celebrity ecosystem (Ferriss, Robbins, Mark Cuban).
Content marketing engine — every contestant generated case study material.
Distributed proof — thousands of merchants publicly building Shopify stores, broadcasting their progress on social media, naturally creating word-of-mouth.

Mechanism of success.

Proof at scale. Instead of one case study, the competition produced 10,000+ live case studies per cohort. Every contestant was a billboard.
Aspirational marketing without spokesperson cost. Robbins/Ferriss endorsement was attached to outcomes, not paid promotion. The endorsement felt earned, not bought.
Inverted funnel. Most marketing funnels are awareness → consideration → purchase. This funnel was: purchase (sign up for Shopify to enter) → awareness (build store, tell network) → consideration (your network considers Shopify). Customers were acquired before they were aware of needing the product.

Outcomes. Competition contestants generated $100M+ in GMV during competition periods alone. Shopify grew from 11,000 merchants (2010) to 1M+ (2017). Currently 4.6M merchants.
Extracted playbook. When you can't differentiate the product, differentiate the path to customer success. Manufacturing proof at scale beats traditional testimonials. Works when (a) your product has measurable success outcomes, (b) you can offer meaningful incentives, (c) you can stage public progress. Fails when outcomes are slow, private, or hard to measure.

Case 6: Tesla — Model 3 Reservation Campaign (2016)
Context. Tesla had Model S ($80K+) and Model X ($90K+) — luxury EVs. Mass market was untouchable. Cash-strapped (this was pre-Model 3 production hell). Needed proof of demand AND working capital simultaneously to fund factory expansion.
Strategy. Charge for the right to wait. $1,000 fully refundable deposit to reserve a future car. Frame this as the launch event, not a financing mechanism.
Execution.

Single in-person event in Hawthorne, CA (3,000 attendees).
Streaming livestream for global audience.
Reservation system opened during the event.
Musk on stage for 30 minutes with the car. No traditional advertising before, during, or after.

Mechanism of success.

Customer-funded R&D. $400M in reservation deposits in first week. This funded Gigafactory expansion. Customers became investors with no equity.
Pre-order as social proof. Each reservation was public (reservation queue numbers were tracked). Owning a low queue number became status. People preordered who didn't intend to buy — just to participate.
Anchor effect. $35K Model 3 announced after years of $80K+ Tesla pricing. Made the car feel like a deal, despite being expensive for a sedan.

Outcomes. 325,000 reservations in first week — $14B in pre-orders. By 2018, 500,000+ reservations. Model 3 became the best-selling EV in history.
Extracted playbook. Pre-order campaigns convert customers into financiers and into marketing assets simultaneously. Works when (a) you have a category-defining product, (b) you have credibility to be trusted with deposits, (c) the product is delayed enough that pre-orders make financial sense. Fails for commodity products, weak brands, or rapid-availability products.

Case 7: Glossier — Community-Built Brand (2014-2020)
Context. Beauty industry dominated by L'Oréal, Estée Lauder, Procter & Gamble — billion-dollar marketing budgets. Emily Weiss had a beauty blog (Into the Gloss) with 1M readers but no product, no brand, no manufacturing.
Strategy. Reverse the development cycle. Most beauty brands develop product then find audience. Glossier built audience for 4 years first (Into the Gloss blog, 2010-2014), then asked the audience what they wanted, then built only those products.
Execution.

Pre-launch content (4 years of blog content built trust and audience intimacy).
Product survey to existing readers — products designed by community.
Initial product line of 4 SKUs (vs typical 50+) — deliberate scarcity.
Influencer seeding through micro-creators (1K-10K followers) instead of celebrities.
Instagram-first photography and product design.

Mechanism of success.

Pre-existing trust. By the time the first product shipped, Emily Weiss had 4 years of relationship equity with the audience. They didn't need to be convinced to try the product — they'd been waiting for it.
Product as community artifact. Customers felt ownership. The products were theirs, designed by their input. This created a different relationship than typical brand-customer.
Aesthetic ownership. Glossier's pink, soft-focus aesthetic became Instagram's beauty aesthetic. Every customer Instagram post reinforced brand visual identity, generating free distribution.

Outcomes. $0 to $1B valuation by 2020. Cult-status retention rates (50%+ repeat purchase in beauty, where industry average is 15-20%).
Extracted playbook. Build the audience before the product. Trust compounds; product launches don't. Works when (a) you have media/content skills to build audience pre-product, (b) you can wait 2-4 years, (c) the category rewards community ownership feeling. Fails when capital constraints force product launch first or when the founder can't sustain content creation alone.

PART II: SYNTHESISED PATTERNS
After analysing these and dozens of similar high-performers, the recurring mechanisms are:
Pattern 1: Re-categorisation Beats Differentiation
The strongest campaigns don't argue "we're better." They argue "we're not in that category."

Liquid Death exited water → entered "premium tribal beverage"
Tesla exited cars → entered "consumer tech"
iPhone exited phones → entered "pocket computer"

Differentiation is incremental and contestable. Re-categorisation is structural and defensible. Once a customer mentally re-categorises, every competitor is fighting the wrong battle.
Practical application: Before crafting messaging, ask: what category does the customer think they're shopping? If the answer is your industry's standard category, you're in a price war. Find a category re-frame.
Pattern 2: Manufacturing Proof Beats Claiming Quality
Every case study above has the same insight: claims are weak, demonstrated outcomes are strong.

Shopify manufactured 10,000+ public success stories per cohort
DSC manufactured the proof on-camera (founder in warehouse, real product)
Notion manufactured proof through templates (every shared template is proof the product produces value)

Why most marketing fails: Claims compete with claims. Every competitor also claims "best quality" / "easy to use" / "great support." None of it converts. Proof — demonstrated, public, third-party — is structurally non-replicable in the moment.
Practical application: For every claim you want to make, ask: can I demonstrate this instead of saying it? If yes, that's the campaign. If no, the claim isn't worth making.
Pattern 3: The Founder-as-Asset Multiplier
Six of seven cases above had a founder visibly attached to the campaign. This is not coincidence.
A founder on-camera is the highest-trust asset a young company has. It signals:

The company cares enough to be vulnerable
There's accountability (a real person, not a corporation)
The product is good enough that the founder will put their reputation on it

Companies that try to scale without founder-led marketing usually plateau at the point where the brand's trust capital runs out — typically around $5-50M depending on category.
Practical application: Until you're at $10M+ revenue, the founder should be the primary face of marketing. Outsourcing to agencies or hiring CMOs before this point typically reduces conversion, not increases it. The trust premium of a founder-on-camera is worth more than any agency's polish.
Pattern 4: Customer-Generated Distribution > Paid Distribution
In every case above, the highest-leverage growth came from customers generating distribution themselves:

Notion templates shared by users
Glossier customers' Instagram posts
Tesla owners showing off cars
Shopify contestants broadcasting progress

This is not "viral marketing" in the trivial sense. It's a structural property of the product design — the product must produce artifacts customers want to share for reasons of their own (status, identity, helpfulness, financial benefit).
Practical application: Before launching campaigns, ask: what does my product produce that a customer would share without being asked? If nothing, the product is undermarketable. Fix the product before fixing the marketing.
Pattern 5: Constraint as Strategic Asset
Several cases used constraints to create demand:

Tesla: limited reservation queue numbers
iPhone: AT&T exclusivity + limited launch supply
Glossier: 4-SKU launch in a category that normally launches 50+
DSC: founder funding meant content had to be high-leverage

Constraints aren't problems to apologise for — they're filtering mechanisms that increase intentionality and create scarcity.
Practical application: Don't expand the product line until the current line is sold out. Don't expand channels until the current channel is saturated. Constraint signals confidence; expansion signals desperation.
Pattern 6: Pre-Trust Compounds, Acquisition Doesn't
Glossier and Notion both spent 2-4 years building audience before the first transaction. This is the highest-ROI growth strategy in modern marketing.
The mathematics are simple: a customer acquired through paid channels costs the same as the second customer. A customer acquired through accumulated trust capital costs near-zero, and produces additional trust capital as a byproduct.
Most companies optimise for CAC. The strongest companies optimise for trust capital accumulation, which makes CAC irrelevant.
Practical application: Audience-building activities (writing, podcasting, community-building, content) look unprofitable for the first 12-24 months. They become disproportionately profitable in years 3-5 if sustained.
Pattern 7: Funnel Inversion
Most marketing thinks awareness → consideration → conversion → retention.
The strongest campaigns invert this:

Tesla: conversion (deposit) → awareness (showing reservation status)
Shopify: conversion (sign up to compete) → awareness (network sees you competing)
DSC: consideration (90-second video tells whole story) → conversion in same session

Conventional funnels assume the customer needs convincing. Inverted funnels make the customer the marketing asset post-conversion.
Practical application: Design your conversion event so that completing it generates marketing material. Pre-order systems, public progress bars, community badges, shared artifacts — all are conversion mechanisms that double as awareness drivers.
Pattern 8: Identity Signaling > Utility Communication
Notion, Liquid Death, Tesla, and Glossier all sell the same fundamental utility as their competitors. Productivity tools organise notes. Water hydrates. Cars drive. Skincare cleanses.
What customers actually buy is identity association:

"I'm the kind of person who uses Notion"
"I'm the kind of person who drinks Liquid Death"
"I'm the kind of person who drives a Tesla"

Utility is the price of entry. Identity is what justifies the premium.
Practical application: Before describing what your product does, describe what kind of person uses it. If you can't write the second description compellingly, the product doesn't have a market position — it has a feature set.

PART III: THE ATTENTION vs REVENUE DISTINCTION
The single biggest failure mode in modern marketing is confusing attention with revenue.
Campaigns that generate attention:

Provocative content (viral tweets, controversial ads)
Celebrity partnerships
Awards and PR
Bold creative work for portfolio purposes

Campaigns that generate revenue:

Specific to a buyer's purchase moment
Have a clear conversion mechanism
Build trust capital that compounds
Generate marketing assets (proof, artifacts, social signals)

The two overlap rarely. Most "great campaigns" in agency case studies generated attention. The campaigns analysed above generated revenue and attention, but the attention was a byproduct of the revenue mechanism, not the goal.
Diagnostic question: Can you trace a direct line from this campaign tactic to a purchase decision? If yes, it's a revenue campaign. If you can only trace it to "brand awareness" or "engagement," it's an attention campaign — and attention campaigns rarely pay back at the unit economics most businesses need.

PART IV: COMMON FAILURE MODES
Failure Mode 1: Optimising for the Wrong Metric
Companies measure CAC, CPC, CTR, engagement. None of these are revenue. A campaign with $10 CAC and 5% LTV:CAC is worse than a campaign with $100 CAC and 10:1 LTV:CAC. Most teams optimise the visible metric and starve the metric that matters.
Failure Mode 2: Polish Substituting for Substance
Agencies and design-led founders over-invest in production quality on weak messaging. A founder-shot iPhone video with sharp messaging beats a $500K production with vague positioning, every time.
Failure Mode 3: Channel Worship
Believing TikTok / LinkedIn / Instagram / paid search is "the channel" misses that channels are distribution mechanisms, not strategies. The strategy is the message and the offer. Channel changes; strategy compounds.
Failure Mode 4: Calendar-Driven Campaigns
Quarterly campaign cycles produce mediocre work because they're constrained by time, not by readiness. The strongest campaigns above launched when the product/audience/message was ready — sometimes after years of preparation. Calendar-driven marketing forces premature launches.
Failure Mode 5: Generic Targeting
"AI startups, research teams, quant firms, enterprise buyers" is not targeting — it's an industry list. Targeting means writing for one buyer so specifically that other buyers self-identify as adjacent. Generic targeting produces generic conversion.
Failure Mode 6: Confusing Loud with Effective
Marketing leadership rewards loud campaigns (visibility = perceived competence). Quiet campaigns that compound (SEO, content, community) get under-resourced. Most companies kill the campaigns that would have worked because they look small at month 3.

PART V: RECOMMENDATIONS FOR LEAN APAC E-COMMERCE
For a business entering Australia, New Zealand, and SEA markets — the specific recommendations:
1. Audience Before Product
APAC markets have small total addressable buyer populations relative to US/EU. Cold acquisition is expensive (small audiences, premium ad costs in AU especially). Build an audience for 6-12 months pre-launch through technical content, founder presence on LinkedIn/X, and industry-specific community participation. Trust capital in APAC compounds faster than in saturated US markets because the noise floor is lower.
2. Re-categorise Out of Commodity Frames
APAC e-commerce buyers default to comparison shopping (Shopback, ProductReview, Trustpilot, ChoiceAU). If your product appears in the standard category, you're in a price comparison war you can't win against incumbents with logistics scale. Position into a category where comparison doesn't apply.
3. Founder-Led Content as Primary Channel
Australian B2B buyers respond strongly to founder transparency. LinkedIn in APAC is high-trust, low-noise compared to US. A founder posting twice weekly on LinkedIn with specific technical insights will outperform $50K/month in paid advertising for the first 18-24 months.
4. Distribution Through Partnerships, Not Direct Acquisition
APAC operates more on trust networks than US e-commerce. Find 5-10 industry-adjacent businesses with overlapping customer bases. Offer revenue share or value exchange. One good partnership in APAC produces more revenue than $100K of paid acquisition.
5. Manufacture Proof Locally
APAC buyers discount overseas case studies heavily. Australian buyers want Australian case studies. SG buyers want SG case studies. Localise proof aggressively — first 10 customers should be your highest priority content production, not just transactions.
6. Pre-Order Mechanism for High-Value Items
For systems priced $5K+ (TensorWorks territory), reservation/deposit mechanisms work exceptionally well in APAC. Australian B2B buyers respond to "secure your unit" framing because supply chain anxiety is high post-COVID.
7. Don't Scale Channels Until They're Saturated
A common APAC mistake: try LinkedIn, try Google Ads, try TikTok, try LinkedIn newsletter, all in 6 months. Pick one channel. Saturate it. Master it. Only then add a second. The strongest APAC e-commerce companies (Canva early days, Atlassian early days) had ruthless channel focus.
8. Build Community, Not Following
A 500-person Slack community of engaged buyers in your category beats 50,000 LinkedIn followers. APAC markets are small enough that 500 right people = market dominance. Optimise for depth.
9. Operate in Specific Geographies, Not "APAC"
"APAC" doesn't exist as a market — it's seven distinct markets. Australia ≠ Singapore ≠ Japan ≠ Indonesia. Pick one. Dominate it. Then expand. Companies trying to launch in three APAC markets simultaneously almost always fail.
10. Optimise for LTV, Not CAC
For TensorWorks specifically: B2B hardware has long sales cycles, high transaction values, low repeat frequency, but enormous repeat expansion potential (customer's first $50K order can become $500K over 3 years). All marketing should be designed to convert customers who become 5-year relationships, not one-transaction buyers. This means: invest in onboarding, technical support, account management — which look like cost centers but are actually the highest-ROI marketing investments in your business.

DIAGNOSTIC CHECKLIST FOR ANY CAMPAIGN
Before launching any campaign, answer:

What specific buyer am I writing for? (Not industry — buyer.)
What category does that buyer think they're shopping in? Can I re-categorise?
What proof can I show instead of claim?
Does completing the conversion event generate marketing material for the next customer?
Can the founder be on-camera credibly?
What identity does the buyer signal by purchasing?
Am I optimising for attention or revenue? How do I know?
What's the trust capital this campaign builds (not just the transactions it drives)?
Is this a calendar-driven campaign or a readiness-driven campaign?
If this works, does the next campaign get easier or harder?

If you can't answer these crisply, the campaign isn't ready.

CLOSING NOTE
The pattern across every analysed case is the same: the campaigns that worked weren't marketing campaigns. They were business model campaigns disguised as marketing.
Dollar Shave Club's subscription model made the video possible. Tesla's reservation mechanism made the launch possible. Notion's product architecture made the templates possible. The marketing was downstream of the business model design.
For revenue-focused operators, the implication is: most marketing problems are actually product, pricing, or business model problems. Diagnose at the right level before reaching for marketing solutions.
The marketing teams that consistently win operate as commercial strategists who happen to communicate, not communicators who execute strategy from above.
